content:
Interview Low Level Design Programming Coding System Design 4 min read Just now -- Press enter or click to view image in full size Photo by Emily Morter on UnsplashWhen most developers begin preparing for Low-Level Design interviews, they jump straight into learning classes, objects, inheritance, and design patterns. I almost did the same. But I quickly realized something. Before learning Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), you need to understand what problem it was trying to solve. Because if you don’t understand why OOP exists, every design pattern and every interview question feels like something you simply have to memorize. Today, we’ll answer a simple question: If most JavaScript and TypeScript code looks functional, why do LLD interviews revolve around OOP? The Code I Write Every Day As a backend developer working with NestJS and TypeScript, my code often looks like this. const revenue = orders .filter(order => order.status === "PAID") .map(order => order.total) .reduce((sum, total) => sum + total, 0);Or this. users .filter(user => user.isActive) .sort(...) .map(...)No classes. No inheritance. No polymorphism. Just functions. So naturally I wondered… Why am I suddenly expected to design everything using classes in an LLD interview? Functional Programming Thinks in Operations Imagine you’re building a small application that manages products. A functional approach separates the data from the operations performed on it. Product -------- name price stockFunctions: createProduct() updatePrice() addStock() displayProduct()Example: const product = { name: "Laptop", price: 70000, stock: 10 };function updatePrice(product, price) { product.price = price; } function addStock(product, quantity) { product.stock += quantity; } Every function receives the product and performs an operation on it. The data and the logic are separate. Object-Oriented Programming Thinks in Things Instead of asking, “What functions do I need?” OOP asks, “What objects exist in my system?” The same product becomes: class Product { String name; double price; int stock; void updatePrice(double newPrice) { this.price = newPrice; } void addStock(int quantity) { stock += quantity; } }Usage becomes much more natural. product.updatePrice(75000); product.addStock(5);The object owns both its state and its behavior. Neither Approach Is Better This is probably the biggest misconception. Many developers assume OOP replaced functional programming. It didn’t. They solve different problems. Functional programming is excellent when you’re transforming data. Examples include: • Filtering collections • Sorting • Aggregating values • Parsing JSON • Data pipelines These operations are naturally expressed as functions. Object-Oriented Programming shines when you’re modeling a business. Think about an online shopping application. It contains concepts like: • User • Product • Cart • Order • Payment • Inventory These are real-world entities. Each has its own data. Each has its own responsibilities. That’s exactly what classes are designed to represent. Why Large Systems Naturally Become Object-Oriented Imagine building Amazon using only standalone functions. Very quickly your codebase starts looking like this. createOrder() cancelOrder() updateOrder() createProduct() updateProduct() refundPayment() processPayment() calculateInventory() ...After hundreds of functions, questions start appearing. • Which function belongs to which feature? • Who owns an Order? • What is allowed to modify a Payment? • Where should validation happen? The project becomes difficult to navigate. Now compare that with an object-oriented design. Order Product Customer Inventory PaymentEach class manages its own data and exposes only the operations that make sense. Responsibilities become clear. This is why OOP scales much better for large business applications. But React Uses Functions… Absolutely. React is solving a completely different problem. A React component simply converts state into UI. function UserCard({ user }) { return

{user.name}

; }Given the same input, it produces the same output. That’s a perfect use case for functional programming. React isn’t modeling a business domain. It’s rendering a user interface. Your Backend Is Already Object-Oriented If you’ve worked with NestJS, you’ve already been writing OOP without thinking about it. A typical application looks like this. Controller ↓ Service ↓ Repository ↓ DatabaseEvery one of these is a class. @Injectable() export class UserService { }@Entity() export class User {}Even though the language supports functional programming, the architecture itself is object-oriented. Modern Applications Use Both This is the key takeaway from today’s lesson. A production application isn’t purely functional. Nor is it purely object-oriented. It uses both. For example: class OrderService { calculateRevenue() { return orders .filter(order => order.status === "PAID") .map(order => order.total) .reduce((sum, total) => sum + total, 0); } }The architecture is object-oriented. The implementation uses functional programming where it makes the code cleaner. That’s exactly how modern software is built. Why LLD Interviews Focus on OOP When an interviewer asks you to design: • Uber • BookMyShow • Splitwise • Parking Lot • Chess They aren’t evaluating your ability to write map() or filter(). They’re evaluating whether you can identify the right entities. Can you model the business? Can you define responsibilities? Can you decide which object owns which behavior? Those are object-oriented design skills. Today’s Takeaway One simple rule will help throughout your LLD journey: If I’m transforming data, I think functionally. If I’m modeling a business, I think in objects. Understanding this distinction is the foundation of Low-Level Design. Next, we’ll build on this by learning the most fundamental concepts in OOP.
